title: Locked-in syndrome: bilateral ventral pons
vignette: A 56-year-old man is brought to the emergency department due to weakness and difficulty speaking. He has a history of hypertension, cigarette smoking, and polysubstance use. The patient experienced a headache while smoking crack cocaine the previous night. This morning, he was found on the bed unable to move or speak. On physical examination, the patient is awake with normal pupillary responses. Vertical eye movements are intact, but horizontal eye movements are impaired. There is loss of motor strength in the upper and lower extremities bilaterally. Ischemic infarction from cocaine-induced vasospasm is suspected. A lesion involving which of the brain regions is the most likely cause of this patient's current condition?

clues.note: Cocaine causes vasospasm and is a stroke risk in young and middle-aged patients. Here it precipitated a basilar-territory (pontine) ischemic infarct.
Quadriplegia plus loss of speech (anarthria) with the patient AWAKE is the core of locked-in syndrome - the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts are severed in the ventral pons.

clues.text: the patient is awake with normal pupillary responses
clues.note: Preserved consciousness and pupillary responses mean the reticular formation and midbrain are spared. This separates locked-in syndrome from coma and from a midbrain lesion.

clues.text: Vertical eye movements are intact, but horizontal eye movements are impaired
clues.note: Vertical gaze (midbrain) is preserved while horizontal gaze (pontine PPRF) is lost - this localizes the lesion to the PONS. Retained vertical eye movement/blinking is how locked-in patients communicate.

clues.text: loss of motor strength in the upper and lower extremities bilaterally
clues.note: Bilateral limb paralysis = both corticospinal tracts, which run together in the ventral (basal) pons - a single midline pontine infarct can hit both.

whatMatters: Awake + quadriplegic + anarthric + horizontal gaze lost but vertical gaze/consciousness preserved = locked-in syndrome from a BILATERAL VENTRAL PONS infarct (corticospinal + corticobulbar tracts), here from cocaine vasospasm in the basilar territory. Vertical gaze and consciousness are spared because the midbrain and reticular formation are above the lesion. Answer D.
strategy: Localize by what's LOST vs SPARED. Lost: limbs, speech, horizontal gaze. Spared: consciousness, vertical gaze, pupils, sensation. The horizontal-gaze center (PPRF) + corticospinal/corticobulbar tracts together = ventral pons. Preserved consciousness rules out the midbrain reticular formation; preserved vertical gaze rules out a midbrain lesion. Medulla lesions give crossed findings (tongue, Horner, Wallenberg), not pure quadriplegia with locked-in features.

eliminations.label: Angular gyrus - cortical, gives Gerstmann
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a dominant angular gyrus lesion causes Gerstmann syndrome (agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, left-right disorientation). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that is a cortical cognitive syndrome, not quadriplegia with locked-in features. ✅ Correct if: can't write, can't calculate, finger agnosia, L-R confusion.

eliminations.label: Cingulate gyrus - abulia, not paralysis
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: cingulate lesions cause behavioral changes such as abulia (lack of will/initiative). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: no quadriplegia, anarthria, or gaze findings. ✅ Correct if: profound apathy / akinetic mutism from a medial frontal lesion.

eliminations.label: Ventral midbrain - Weber, and consciousness/gaze would be hit
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: ventral midbrain (Weber syndrome) gives ipsilateral CN III palsy with contralateral hemiparesis; the midbrain also houses vertical-gaze centers and the reticular formation. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this patient has PRESERVED vertical gaze, pupils, and consciousness, so the midbrain is spared; the deficits localize lower, to the pons. ✅ Correct if: a down-and-out, blown-pupil eye with contralateral weakness.

eliminations.label: Medial medulla - crossed signs, tongue
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: medial medullary syndrome causes contralateral arm/leg weakness, contralateral dorsal-column loss, and ipsilateral tongue deviation (CN XII). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is usually unilateral and doesn't produce locked-in features or a horizontal-gaze palsy with preserved consciousness. ✅ Correct if: hemiparesis sparing the face + tongue deviation + proprioception loss.

eliminations.label: Lateral medulla - Wallenberg, sensory/cerebellar
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: lateral medullary (Wallenberg/PICA) syndrome gives vertigo/nystagmus, ipsilateral facial + contralateral body pain/temp loss, ataxia, dysphagia, and ipsilateral Horner. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it spares strength and doesn't cause quadriplegia or locked-in syndrome. ✅ Correct if: crossed pain/temp loss with ataxia, hoarseness, and Horner.

reasoning: Awake, quadriplegic, unable to speak, horizontal gaze lost but vertical gaze/consciousness/pupils preserved = locked-in syndrome
Corticospinal (limbs) + corticobulbar (speech) tracts and the horizontal-gaze center (PPRF) all sit in the ventral pons
Vertical gaze and consciousness are preserved because the midbrain and reticular formation are above the lesion
Cocaine vasospasm caused a bilateral ventral pontine (basilar-territory) infarct -> answer D
mechanism: Locked-in syndrome results from a lesion of the bilateral ventral (basal) pons, classically a basilar-artery territory infarct - here precipitated by cocaine-induced vasospasm. The ventral pons carries the corticospinal tracts (voluntary limb movement) and corticobulbar tracts (voluntary control of lower cranial-nerve motor function, including speech), so their bilateral interruption produces quadriplegia and anarthria. The paramedian pontine reticular formation (the horizontal-gaze center) is also damaged, abolishing horizontal eye movements. Consciousness is preserved because the midbrain/upper-pontine reticular formation is spared, and vertical gaze and eyelid elevation persist because they are controlled in the rostral midbrain - allowing these patients to communicate by vertical eye movements and blinking. Sensation and brainstem/spinal reflexes remain intact because the dorsal/tegmental sensory pathways are not involved.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Neuro > brainstem stroke syndromes: locked-in (bilateral ventral pons; corticospinal + corticobulbar + PPRF); medial medullary, lateral medullary (Wallenberg), Weber (ventral midbrain); cortical syndromes (Gerstmann, cingulate).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Localize by lost vs spared: lost limbs/speech/horizontal gaze, spared consciousness/vertical gaze/pupils/sensation -> bilateral ventral pons. The ask is the LOCATION, not the named syndrome.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on locked-in syndrome, corticospinal vs corticobulbar tracts, PPRF/horizontal gaze, reticular formation, Wallenberg, Weber, or Gerstmann? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Ventral midbrain (C) tempts via 'brainstem,' but preserved vertical gaze, pupils, and consciousness put the lesion BELOW the midbrain, in the pons.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The brainstem cross-section shows the ventral pons carrying the CS/CB tracts and PPRF (lost) vs the spared dorsal/midbrain structures (vertical gaze, reticular formation, sensory tracts). If you can map 'horizontal gaze + limbs + speech lost, vertical gaze + consciousness kept' onto that diagram, it lands on ventral pons.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: locked-in = bilateral VENTRAL PONS; awake + quadriplegic + only vertical eye movements.

keywords.front: Awake, quadriplegic, anarthric, only vertical eye movements

keywords.back: Wallenberg: crossed pain/temp, ataxia, Horner, dysphagia
Weber: CN III palsy + contralateral hemiparesis

firstAid.detail: Neuro > bilateral ventral pons infarct (basilar a.); corticospinal + corticobulbar + PPRF; consciousness & vertical gaze preserved.
firstAid.location: Neuro > bilateral ventral pons infarct (basilar a.); corticospinal + corticobulbar + PPRF; consciousness & vertical gaze preserved.

firstAid.detail: Neuro > Weber (ventral midbrain), medial & lateral medullary (Wallenberg), Gerstmann (angular gyrus), cingulate/abulia.
firstAid.location: Neuro > Weber (ventral midbrain), medial & lateral medullary (Wallenberg), Gerstmann (angular gyrus), cingulate/abulia.

firstAid.detail: Neuro/Pharm > cocaine vasospasm -> ischemic stroke; basilar territory -> pons.
firstAid.location: Neuro/Pharm > cocaine vasospasm -> ischemic stroke; basilar territory -> pons.

memoryHook: Locked-IN, eyes UP: a bilateral ventral pons infarct traps the body but leaves the patient awake, blinking and looking up to talk.
